misconceptionSummary: Students do not understand what a remainder means in a real-world context — they do not know whether to round up, round down, or express as a fraction.

visuals.ruleText: What you do with a remainder depends entirely on the real-world situation
visuals.subText: Sometimes round up. Sometimes round down. Sometimes express as a fraction.

steps.narration: The arithmetic gives a remainder — but what that remainder means depends on the situation. Knowing when to round up, round down, or express as a fraction is not a maths rule; it is about understanding the problem.

lines.text: 13 people, minibuses hold 4 — how many buses?
Need 4 buses — round UP to fit everyone  ✓

steps.narration: Thirteen people in minibuses of four: three remainder one. You cannot leave one person behind, so you need a fourth bus — round up to four. The remainder forces an extra whole unit.

lines.text: 13 apples shared equally between 4 — how many each?
3 apples each — round DOWN, 1 left over  ✓

steps.narration: Thirteen apples shared equally between four people: three remainder one. Each person gets three whole apples. The one left over cannot be divided in this context — round down to three. Same arithmetic, opposite decision.

visuals.label: Same remainder — different interpretation depending on context

steps.narration: Three remainder one — but four buses or three apples, depending on the situation. Reading the problem carefully determines how to handle the remainder.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: always rounding down when there is a remainder. Sometimes the remainder forces rounding up (e.g. needing an extra bus). Always ask: what does the remainder mean in this situation?
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: The remainder is not automatically dropped. Ask what it means in context: does the leftover force an extra unit (round up), or is it simply unused (round down)? The context decides.
